answers.value: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice considerations related to proximity to State Route 99 and the Union Pacific rail corridor; construction-period community disruption (dust, truck traffic, and temporary detours); long-term maintenance responsibilities for private vs. public open space; and climate change resilience, including shade tree planting, heat island reduction through reflective roofing and paving materials, and emergency evacuation access during regional flooding events.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Present Land Use Zoning General Plan Designation
answers.value: The approximately 118.5-acre project site is located within the City of Riverbend’s Sphere of Influence but remains in unincorporated Butte County. Existing County zoning is AG-20 (Agriculture, 20-acre minimum) with a General Plan designation of Agriculture/Open Space. Current on-site uses consist of irrigated pasture historically used for seasonal cattle grazing, a small orchard area that has been largely fallowed over the last five years, and two older single-family residences with associated barns and sheds clustered near the northwest corner of the site. Surrounding land uses include low-density single-family residential subdivisions to the west within City limits (zoned R-1 and R-2), neighborhood commercial and office uses along East Riverbend Boulevard to the southwest (zoned C-1 and C-2), additional agricultural lands to the north and east in the County, and the Union Pacific rail corridor and State Route 99 further west beyond the existing residential neighborhoods. The project proposes to annex the site into the City and redesignate it under the City’s General Plan as Mixed Use (MU) with zoning of Planned Development (PD) with subdistricts for Medium Density Residential, High Density Residential, Mixed-Use Commercial, and Open Space/Parks.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan (project) is a comprehensive, master-planned community intended to provide a walkable, transit-supportive mixed-use neighborhood on approximately 118.5 acres at the eastern edge of the City of Riverbend. The project would transition the site from underutilized agricultural land and scattered rural residential uses to a compact, pedestrian-oriented village that integrates housing, neighborhood-serving commercial and office uses, parks, and multi-modal transportation improvements.

At full buildout, the project would allow development of up to 420 residential dwelling units in a range of product types, including:

- Approximately 220 medium-density single-family detached homes on small lots (averaging 4,000–5,000 square feet), many of which would be alley-loaded to minimize driveway dominance along the streetscape.
- Approximately 120 townhomes and duplex units clustered around shared green courts and close to the neighborhood park and commercial core.
- Up to 80 multi-family units in two- to three-story buildings located above ground-floor commercial space within the mixed-use village center and in one stand-alone apartment complex near the transit stop.

Non-residential components would include approximately 80,000 square feet of office space and 65,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial uses. The office space is anticipated to accommodate professional services (such as small medical offices, accountants, and engineering firms), co-working space, and local business headquarters. The commercial space would be focused on daily needs and community-serving retail and services, including a small-format grocery store (roughly 20,000 square feet), cafes and restaurants, a fitness studio, personal services (hair salons, dry cleaners, etc.), and potentially a childcare facility. Together, these uses are intended to reduce vehicle miles traveled by allowing residents to meet many daily needs within walking or biking distance of their homes.

The project includes a robust parks and open space system totaling approximately 12.1 acres. This system would consist of a centrally located 6-acre neighborhood park with play equipment, multi-use turf fields, picnic areas, a small amphitheater lawn for community events, and restrooms; several smaller pocket parks distributed throughout the residential neighborhoods; and a linear greenway with a Class I multi-use trail along Riverbend Creek and the on-site stormwater detention basins. The project also proposes a 10,000-square-foot community center building adjacent to the neighborhood park, which would include a large multi-purpose room for community meetings and classes, a small kitchen, flexible rooms that could be used for after-school programs or senior activities, and office space for a neighborhood association or non-profit partner.

Transportation improvements are a key component of the Specific Plan. The internal circulation system would provide a connected network of local streets with sidewalks on both sides, traffic-calming features such as curb extensions and raised crosswalks near parks and schools, and dedicated Class II bike lanes on the primary neighborhood collectors. A Class I multi-use path would run generally north–south through the site along the creek corridor and connect to existing and planned regional trails, providing safe, separated bicycle and pedestrian access to Riverbend High School, East Riverbend Elementary School, and the existing commercial areas along East Riverbend Boulevard. The project would construct a new signalized intersection at East Riverbend Boulevard and County Road 17, which would serve as the primary vehicular access point and include marked crosswalks, bicycle detection, and transit stop enhancements (shelters, benches, and lighting) for the existing Butte Regional Transit route. Two additional secondary access points would be provided on County Road 17 to ensure adequate emergency access and circulation.

Infrastructure improvements would include extension and upsizing of existing City water and sewer lines from the west, construction of on-site water, sewer, and storm drain networks, and installation of stormwater detention and water quality treatment basins integrated into the open space network. The project would connect to the City’s municipal water supply and regional wastewater treatment plant; no new independent water or wastewater treatment facilities are proposed. Stormwater facilities are designed to meet current low-impact development (LID) standards, incorporating bioswales, permeable paving in select parking areas, and vegetated basins to capture and treat runoff before it is discharged to Riverbend Creek.

Architecturally, the project would feature a mix of contemporary craftsman, farmhouse, and Spanish-influenced styles, with an emphasis on front porches, varied rooflines, and high-quality materials to provide visual interest along the streets. The Specific Plan includes design guidelines addressing building massing, articulation, roof forms, façade treatments, and landscaping to promote a cohesive village character while allowing for variety among builders. Residential neighborhoods are arranged so that most homes are within a five-minute walk of a park or open space area, and larger blocks are broken up with pedestrian paseos.

The project also incorporates a number of sustainability features. These include a requirement that all homes be pre-wired for electric vehicle charging, installation of rooftop solar photovoltaic systems on the community center and encouraged on residential and commercial buildings, a minimum tree canopy coverage goal to reduce urban heat island effects, and water-efficient landscaping and irrigation systems that comply with the State’s Model Water Efficient Landscape Ordinance. The Specific Plan encourages building designs that can achieve at least 10 percent greater energy efficiency than Title 24 baseline standards at the time of construction and includes guidance for orienting buildings to maximize natural light and passive cooling.

Implementation of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan will require a series of discretionary approvals, including City adoption of the Specific Plan, a General Plan amendment to redesignate the site from Agriculture/Open Space to Mixed Use, a rezone to Planned Development with associated development standards, approval of a Planned Unit Development overlay, a vesting tentative subdivision map to create the necessary residential, commercial, and open space parcels, site plan and architectural review for the non-residential and multi-family components, and approval of a Development Agreement between the City and the project applicant. Construction is anticipated to occur in multiple phases over approximately 8 to 12 years, with initial phases focusing on backbone infrastructure, the neighborhood park and community center, and the first residential neighborhoods closest to existing City services. Later phases would complete the mixed-use commercial core and the remaining residential areas as market demand warrants.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
